data.title: Lyft boosts its presence in Europe through the acquisition of FREENOW
data.description:
Lyft (NASDAQ:LYFT) announced on Thursday that it has completed the $197 million acquisition of European multi-mobility taxi app FREENOW. The Lyft (NASDAQ:LYFT) app now prompts riders to download FREENOW when opened in FREENOW’s 180 cities across nine countries in Europe.

The acquisition gives Lyft (LYFT) an immediate presence in nine European countries and over 150 cities, including major hubs such as London, Berlin, Madrid, Paris, and Rome—markets where FREENOW is a leader. The deal nearly doubles Lyft's (LYFT) total addressable market to over 300 billion personal vehicle trips per year. 

FREENOW’s strength in digital taxi and multi-modal services (including e-scooters and car-sharing) was noted to complement Lyft's (LYFT) rideshare business and help to diversify the company's revenue streams.

"Every conversation I've had with FREENOW drivers and team members has reminded me why we’re joining forces: to bring the best of each company to the other," stated Lyft CEO David Risher. "This isn't just about growing our business. It's about serving and connecting people worldwide," he added.

FREENOW CEO Thomas Zimmermann and his leadership team will report to Jeremy Bird, EVP of Driver Experience at Lyft. The FREENOW country general managers will continue to strategically lead operations in Ireland, the United Kingdom, Germany, Greece, Spain, Italy, Poland, France, and Austria.

The acquisition will not impact Lyft's (LYFT) Q2 financial results.

data.title: SNDL stock jumps as income turns positive for the first time
data.description:
Shares of SNDL (NASDAQ:SNDL) jumped 8.3% premarket on Thursday after the Canadian marijuana player reported second quarter results financials showing revenue growth and its first-ever positive operating income.

The Calgary-headquartered firm posted C$244.8 million in net revenue, up 7.3% from the same period last year. The growth was mainly driven by a 17.4% jump in the combined Cannabis business and a return to growth in the Liquor retail segment.

The company reported positive operating income of C$5M in the quarter, which includes a -C$0.8 million restructuring charge. Excluding this charge, adjusted operating income totaled C$5.8 million.

Net earnings totaled C$2.88M, compared to a loss of C$4.96M in the prior year quarter.

“Achieving positive quarterly operating income and net earnings across both metrics for the first time in the company's history during Q2 2025 marks a pivotal milestone and underscores the effectiveness of our strategic improvement agenda,” SNDL said.

Gross margin in Q2 stood at 27.6%, matching the record set in the first quarter and marking a 2.1 percentage point increase compared to the same period last year.

However, free cash flow in the second quarter of 2025 was negative at $(7.9) million, as strong P&L performance was outweighed by working capital investments to support international growth in the second half of the year, annual incentive and insurance payments, and capital expenditures for upcoming store openings.

data.title: HONEST Act: Should members of Congress be allowed to trade stocks?
data.description:
While the congressional stock trading question has played out on Capitol Hill for years, the debate saw some renewed momentum this week. A bill sponsored by Senator Josh Hawley (R-MO) has cleared the Senate Homeland Security and Government Affairs Committee by an 8-7 vote along party lines, with all Republicans voting against the measure except for Hawley. GOP members cited several different reasons for their disapproval, including rushed legislation, discouraging some from holding office, concessions to the executive branch, or just another way target the White House.

Snapshot: Members of Congress have a lot of privileged and classified information that could move stock prices (think back to COVID pandemic), as well as financial incentives from companies that routinely lobby Congress. Those decisions could also play a role in how much a given stock is worth, and Congress sought to counteract that in 2012 by passing a bill known as the STOCK Act. While the legislation requires lawmakers to disclose trades within 45 days, many say it doesn't do enough to prevent insider trading and conflicts of interest.

The new bill by Hawley is similar to the PELOSI Act he introduced in 2023, though that one didn't make it out of committee. While President Trump initially expressed interest in the bill, his tone changed after the partisan divide, as well as Hawley's vote against an amendment that would have investigated Nancy Pelosi's past stock trades. Senate Majority Leader John Thune will now have to decide whether to bring the bill to a floor vote, but the chances of it moving forward are looking slim.

What's in the bill: The new measure, called the HONEST Act, would ban members of Congress and their spouses from trading stocks while in office, requiring them to gradually divest their shares. It would also apply to the president and vice president, but that provision would only apply to an elected official's next term (meaning after President Trump and JD Vance leave office). The bill would also seek to close other loopholes surrounding trades, as well as written certification requirements and compliance audits.

data.title: Belden beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; initiates Q3 outlook
data.description:
• Belden press release (NYSE:BDC): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.89 beats by $0.13.
  • Revenue of $671.99M (+11.2% Y/Y) beats by $14.84M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $114 million, up $15 million, or 15%, compared to $99 million in the year-ago period. Adjusted EBITDA margin was 17.0%, up 50 bps, compared to 16.5% in the year-ago period. 

  • The table below provides guidance for the third quarter of 2025.

    Third Quarter 2025:

    Guidance

    Revenues (million)

    $670 - $685 vs. $677.53M consensus

    GAAP EPS

    $1.33 - $1.43

    Adjusted EPS

    $1.85 - $1.95 vs. $1.84 consensus

data.title: APi Group beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; initiates Q3 and raises FY25 outlook
data.description:
• APi Group press release (NYSE:APG): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.39 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $2B (+15.6% Y/Y) beats by $100M.

  • Record second quarter adjusted EBITDA of $272 million with year-over-year growth of 17.7% and adjusted EBITDA margin expansion of 30 basis points to a record 13.7%.

  • Raising full-year guidance for net revenues and adjusted EBITDA

  • Guidance

    APi increases its full-year 2025 guidance for net revenue and adjusted EBITDA.

    • Net Revenues of $7,650 to $7,850 million, up from $7,400 to $7,600 million vs. $7.52B consensus.
    • Adjusted EBITDA of $1,005 to $1,045 million, up from $985 to $1,035 million.
    • Adjusted Free Cash Flow Conversion of approximately 75%.

    APi announces its guidance for the third quarter of 2025.

    • Net Revenues of $1,985 to $2,035 million vs. $1.94B consensus.
    • Adjusted EBITDA of $270 to $280 million.

data.title: PPL Non-GAAP EPS of $0.32 misses by $0.07, revenue of $2.03B beats by $40M
data.description:
• PPL press release (NYSE:PPL): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.32 misses by $0.07.
  • Revenue of $2.03B (+8.0% Y/Y) beats by $40M.
  • Reaffirms 2025 ongoing earnings forecast range of $1.75 to $1.87 per share; expects to achieve at least the midpoint of $1.81 per share.
  • Reaffirms 6% to 8% annual EPS and dividend growth targets through at least 2028; expects to achieve EPS growth in the top half of targeted growth range.

data.title: UK regulator says Amazon, Microsoft impacting cloud competition
data.description:
U.K.'s competition regulator said that Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) are hurting competition in the cloud computing industry and called for a review into their market dominance under the country’s new tech rules.

The Competition and Markets Authority said Microsoft and Amazon Web Services, or AWS, "have been generating sustained returns from their cloud services substantially above their cost of capital in cloud services for a number of years."

"The Inquiry Group’s final report disregards clear evidence of robust competition in the UK’s IT services industry, which cloud computing has revolutionised by dramatically reducing costs and expanding customer choice and flexibility. The action proposed by the Inquiry Group is unwarranted and undermines the substantial investment and innovation that have already benefitted hundreds of thousands of UK businesses," said an AWS spokesperson in an emailed statement to Seeking Alpha.

"It risks making the UK a global outlier at a time when businesses need regulatory predictability for the UK to maintain international competitiveness. We will continue to engage constructively with the CMA as they consider their next steps," the AWS spokesperson added.

Microsoft did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

The agency also said that Microsoft’s licensing practices are adversely impacting the competitiveness of AWS and Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google in the supply of cloud services, particularly in competing for customers that buy cloud services which use the relevant Microsoft software as an input.

"As a result, Microsoft faces weaker competitive constraints from AWS and Google, its most significant competitors, which is reducing competition in cloud services markets," the CMA added.

This gives rise to an adverse effects on competition, or AEC, in cloud services in the U.K., according to the regulator.

The watchdog added that market concentration, which is particularly high in the case of infrastructure as a service or IaaS, and barriers to entry and expansion are features that have enabled the two largest providers, Microsoft and AWS, to hold significant unilateralmarket power in the cloud services markets and to earn returns above the cost of their capital over a sustained period.

The CMA noted that these features give rise to an AEC, in cloud services in the U.K. because it is harder for alternative cloud suppliers to enter and grow in these markets and customers face a limited choice of suppliers and products.

The agency said that technical and commercial barriers are features that give rise to an AEC in cloud services in the U.K. "These barriers lock customers into their initial choice of provider, which may not reflect their evolving needs and limits their ability to exercise choice of cloud provider," the CMA added.

As per the CMA, Microsoft and AWS, each has a high share of supply at about 30% to 40% in the IaaS market in 2024. The platform as a service, or PaaS, market is also concentrated; Microsoft’s share of it has slightly increased to 20% to 30% and AWS’ share has remained stable at 10% to 20% since 2020.

The third largest provider, Google, has a much lower share of supply in both IaaS and PaaS markets at [5% to10%] but this has grown since 2020, the agency noted.

The CMA recommended that the "CMA Board use its digital markets powers to prioritise commencing SMS investigations to consider designating the two largest providers Microsoft and AWS with strategic market status (SMS) in relation to their respective digital activities in cloud services."

Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers, or DMCC, Act was  introduced in the U.K. with the aim to prevent anti-competitive behavior in digital markets.

The CMA said that it will keep under review possible options for further SMS designation investigations, and it expects that options will be considered by the CMA Board in early 2026.

Update: The story was updated with a statement from Amazon's AWS.
